shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: And finally, random acts of kindness are conscious acts of kindness. We get people, when they open up their inbox, to write one positive email praising or thanking somebody in their support network.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 最後，隨機的善意行為是有意識的善意行為。當人們打開收件匣時，我們會讓他們寫一封積極的電子郵件，讚揚或感謝他們支持網路中的某人。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: And by doing these activities and by training your brain just like we train our bodies, what we've found is we can reverse the formula for happiness and success,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 透過進行這些活動並像訓練我們的身體一樣訓練您的大腦，我們發現我們可以扭轉幸福和成功的公式，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: and in doing so, not only create ripples of positivity, but a real revolution. Thank you very much. (Applause)
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 這樣做不僅會產生正面的漣漪，而且會引發一場真正的革命。非常感謝。 （掌聲）

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: I am a writer. Writing books is my profession but it's more than that, of course. It is also my great lifelong love and fascination. And I don't expect that that's ever going to change.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我是個作家 寫作是我的專業，但當然，不僅如此 這也是我一輩子的最愛與迷戀 所以我也沒想過這會有所改變

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: But, that said, something kind of peculiar has happened recently in my life and in my career, which has caused me to have to recalibrate my whole relationship with this work.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 但，說到這，最近有件相當奇特的事發生了 在我的生命與事業生涯中 這讓我重新審查我與這份工作間的整個關係

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And the peculiar thing is that I recently wrote this book, this memoir called "Eat, Pray, Love" which, decidedly unlike any of my previous books,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 而這特別的事就是我最近寫了這本書 這本回憶錄叫做"飲食，禱告，愛" 這本書很明顯地，跟我之前的書很不一樣

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: went out in the world for some reason, and became this big, mega-sensation, international bestseller thing. The result of which is that everywhere I go now, people treat me like I'm doomed.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因著某些原因被出版 並成為相當轟動且國際知名的暢銷書 這讓我現在不論到了哪 人們都異樣地對待我，彷彿我已經完蛋了

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Seriously -- doomed, doomed! Like, they come up to me now, all worried, and they say, "Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to top that?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 真的! 玩完了，沒救了！ 像是，他們會往前走向我，並擔心地說著

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Aren't you afraid you're going to keep writing for your whole life and you're never again going to create a book that anybody in the world cares about at all, ever again?"
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你不怕你終身都要一直寫作嗎？ 你不怕你再也無法創作出一本  從此不再

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: So that's reassuring, you know. But it would be worse, except for that I happen to remember that over 20 years ago, when I was a teenager,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 所以這一切都還蠻慶幸的，你也知道的 但情況也可能更糟，要不是我突然記起

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: when I first started telling people that I wanted to be a writer, I was met with this same sort of fear-based reaction. And people would say, "Aren't you afraid you're never going to have any success?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當我第一次告訴人們我想成為作家時，我也遇到了同樣基於恐懼的反應。人們會說：「你不擔心你永遠不會成功嗎？

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Aren't you afraid the humiliation of rejection will kill you? Aren't you afraid that you're going to work your whole life at this craft and nothing's ever going to come of it
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你不怕被拒絕的羞辱會要了你的命嗎？你難道不擔心你將一輩子都在這門手藝上工作卻一事無成嗎

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and you're going to die on a scrap heap of broken dreams with your mouth filled with bitter ash of failure?" (Laughter) Like that, you know.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 諸如此類，你知道的

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: The answer -- the short answer to all those questions is, "Yes." Yes, I'm afraid of all those things. And I always have been. And I'm afraid of many, many more things besides
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這答案，所有問題的答案，就是"是的" 是的，我是害怕著這所有的事情 而且我一直這樣害怕著 而同時也有更多的事令我感到害怕

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: that people can't even guess at, like seaweed and other things that are scary. But, when it comes to writing, the thing that I've been sort of thinking about lately, and wondering about lately, is why?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 像是海草，還有其他可怕的東西 但，當講到寫作這一件事 我最近常想到，並且不斷思索的是"為什麼"

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: You know, is it rational? Is it logical that anybody should be expected to be afraid of the work that they feel they were put on this Earth to do. And what is it specifically about creative ventures
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 應該要為他們覺得在這世上註定做的工作感到害怕 你知道的，創意工作到底有著什麼樣具體的東西

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: that seems to make us really nervous about each other's mental health in a way that other careers kind of don't do, you know? Like my dad, for example, was a chemical engineer
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 似乎用著某種方式，讓我們變得如此緊張擔心於彼此的心靈健康 在別種行業不太會這樣，對嗎？ 舉個例來說，像我父親，他是個化學工程師

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and I don't recall once in his 40 years of chemical engineering anybody asking him if he was afraid to be a chemical engineer, you know? "That chemical-engineering block, John, how's it going?"
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 而你知道嗎？在他四十年的化學工程生涯中，我不曾記得 任何人問過他是否他害怕成為一個化學工程師 並沒有。 化學工程妨礙了約翰嗎？，狀況如何了呢？

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: It just didn't come up like that, you know? But to be fair, chemical engineers as a group haven't really earned a reputation over the centuries for being alcoholic manic-depressives. (Laughter)
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你知道的，事情並沒有變成這樣 但平心而論，化學工程總體來說 在過去的幾個世紀裡，並沒有因酗酒躁鬱症  （笑聲）

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: We writers, we kind of do have that reputation, and not just writers, but creative people across all genres, it seems, have this reputation for being enormously mentally unstable.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我們作家，就有點這樣的聲譽 當然不只是作家，也包含了各領域的創作人才 似乎都有著情緒非常不穩定的惡名

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And all you have to do is look at the very grim death count in the 20th century alone, of really magnificent creative minds who died young and often at their own hands, you know?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你所需做的就是看看光在二十世紀，就有無數偉大的創作靈魂隕落 這是多令人悲痛的死亡 這些藝術家通常早逝且總是死於自己的手上，你知道嗎？

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And even the ones who didn't literally commit suicide seem to be really undone by their gifts, you know. Norman Mailer, just before he died, last interview, he said,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 而即使是那些沒有真的自殺的人 就像你所知的，似乎也因著他們的天賦而被困擾抑鬱著 諾曼˙梅勒，就在他死前的最後一場訪問，他說道

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: "Every one of my books has killed me a little more." An extraordinary statement to make about your life's work. But we don't even blink when we hear somebody say this,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: "每個在我書中出現的角色都扼殺了我一些 你知道的，這是對於你生命中的作品非常特殊的陳述 但當我們聽到有人這樣說時，我們甚至連眼睛都不會眨一下

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: because we've heard that kind of stuff for so long and somehow we've completely internalized and accepted collectively this notion that creativity and suffering are somehow inherently linked
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因為長久以來我們都聽著這類的話語 且不知為何，我們完全將這觀念內化並全面接受 這觀念就是--我們的創造力和所遭受的痛苦息息相關

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and that artistry, in the end, will always ultimately lead to anguish. And the question that I want to ask everybody here today is are you guys all cool with that idea? Are you comfortable with that?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 而其中藝術的本質，到頭來，都會導致心中的苦悶 而今天我想問在場各位的問題是  你覺得這樣妥當嗎？

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Because you look at it even from an inch away and, you know -- I'm not at all comfortable with that assumption. I think it's odious. And I also think it's dangerous,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因為即使是從一吋遠的地方來看這件事  我覺得這是很可恨的 我也認為這是危險的

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and I don't want to see it perpetuated into the next century. I think it's better if we encourage our great creative minds to live. And I definitely know that, in my case -- in my situation --
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 而且我並不想看到這樣的想法延伸到下一個世紀  我當然知道，就我而言，在我的狀況來說

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: it would be very dangerous for me to start sort of leaking down that dark path of assumption, particularly given the circumstance that I'm in right now in my career.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 如果我開始走入剛提的那條黑暗的假設路途，那是相當危險的 尤其是我現在處於這樣的處境中

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Which is -- you know, like check it out, I'm pretty young, I'm only about 40 years old. I still have maybe another four decades of work left in me.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這有點像是，你懂的，你瞧 我相當年輕，我只有大概40歲

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And it's exceedingly likely that anything I write from this point forward is going to be judged by the world as the work that came after the freakish success of my last book, right?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 而很有可能的是我從這一刻起寫的東西 將被世界論斷為我上一部

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: I should just put it bluntly, because we're all sort of friends here now -- it's exceedingly likely that my greatest success is behind me. So Jesus, what a thought!
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我應該直說，因為我們現在在此有點像是朋友 很有可能的是，我最偉大的作品已在我身後了 噢，天啊，這是多糟的一個想法

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: That's the kind of thought that could lead a person to start drinking gin at nine o'clock in the morning, and I don't want to go there. (Laughter) I would prefer to keep doing this work that I love.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你知道的，這是那種想法，會讓一個人   （笑聲）

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And so, the question becomes, how? And so, it seems to me, upon a lot of reflection, that the way that I have to work now, in order to continue writing,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 似乎對我來說，在多次省思後 為了能繼續寫作，我現在必須採取的方式是

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: is that I have to create some sort of protective psychological construct, right? I have to sort of find some way to have a safe distance between me, as I am writing, and my very natural anxiety
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我必須創造出某種保護式的心理架構  在我內心憂慮與我本身間保持安全距離

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: about what the reaction to that writing is going to be, from now on. And, as I've been looking, over the last year, for models for how to do that, I've been sort of looking across time,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 而這憂慮無非來自他人對作品的反應 過去的幾年，為了如何做到，我一直在尋找可以仿效的模式  我依著歷史的時間軸去尋找

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and I've been trying to find other societies to see if they might have had better and saner ideas than we have about how to help creative people
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我也一直在找其他的社會模式 看看是否他們有更好且更合乎情理的想法 讓我們可以知道如何幫助有創作力的人們

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: sort of manage the inherent emotional risks of creativity. And that search has led me to ancient Greece and ancient Rome. So stay with me, because it does circle around and back.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這研究引領我回到古希臘與古羅馬 所以請專心聽我說，因為我們還會繞回來講這個話題

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: But, ancient Greece and ancient Rome -- people did not happen to believe that creativity came from human beings back then, OK? People believed that creativity was this divine attendant spirit
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 並不是湊巧相信創造力 來自於人類 人們相信創造力是種跟隨著人的神聖靈魂

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: that came to human beings from some distant and unknowable source, for distant and unknowable reasons. The Greeks famously called these divine attendant spirits of creativity "daemons."
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這來自於遙遠不可知的源頭，並存於人類 因著遙遠且不可知的原因 著名的希臘人稱這伴隨著人的創造力神聖靈魂為"守護神"

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Socrates, famously, believed that he had a daemon who spoke wisdom to him from afar. The Romans had the same idea, but they called that sort of disembodied creative spirit a genius.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 名聲顯赫的蘇格拉底相信他就有這樣的守護神 會從遠方向他傾瀉智慧 羅馬人也有相同的概念 但他們稱這種虛幻的創作靈為天才

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Which is great, because the Romans did not actually think that a genius was a particularly clever individual. They believed that a genius was this, sort of magical divine entity,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這是很棒的，因為事實上羅馬人並不認為  他們相信天才是一種不可思議並神聖的實體

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: who was believed to literally live in the walls of an artist's studio, kind of like Dobby the house elf, and who would come out and sort of invisibly assist the artist with their work
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 並相信這實體是真的住在藝術家工作室的牆壁中  祂能不為人察覺地出現並幫助藝術家完成作品

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and would shape the outcome of that work. So brilliant -- there it is, right there, that distance that I'm talking about -- that psychological construct to protect you from the results of your work.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這是如此地聰慧！就是這個，這就是我在講的距離

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And everyone knew that this is how it functioned, right? So the ancient artist was protected from certain things, like, for example, too much narcissism, right?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 而大家都知道這是如何運行的，對吧？ 所以古代的藝術家就可以受到保護，不被某些事物影響

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: If your work was brilliant, you couldn't take all the credit for it, everybody knew that you had this disembodied genius who had helped you. If your work bombed, not entirely your fault, you know?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 如果你有著令人讚嘆的作品，你不能完全歸功於你本身  如果你工作不順，這也不完全是你的錯，明白嗎？

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Everyone knew your genius was kind of lame. (Laughter) And this is how people thought about creativity in the West for a really long time. And then the Renaissance came and everything changed,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 大家會知道你內的天才有點拙劣

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and we had this big idea, and the big idea was, let's put the individual human being at the center of the universe above all gods and mysteries, and there's no more room for mystical creatures
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我們有一個偉大的想法，偉大的想法是，讓我們將人類個體置於宇宙的中心，高於所有神靈和神秘事物，不再有神秘生物的空間

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: who take dictation from the divine. And it's the beginning of rational humanism, and people started to believe that creativity came completely from the self of the individual.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 他們接受神的口授。這是理性人文主義的開始，人們開始相信創造力完全來自個人的自我。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And for the first time in history, you start to hear people referring to this or that artist as being a genius, rather than having a genius. And I got to tell you, I think that was a huge error.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你開始聽到人們稱這個或那個藝術家為天才 而非身邊有著天才的靈 而我必須告訴你，我認為這是個極大的錯誤

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: You know, I think that allowing somebody, one mere person to believe that he or she is like, the vessel, you know, like the font and the essence and the source
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你知道的，我認為任憑一個人，就只是一個人 去相信他或她自己就像是個容器 負載著像是所有神聖、有著創造力的、不可知的、永恆的奧祕

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: of all divine, creative, unknowable, eternal mystery is just a smidge too much responsibility to put on one fragile, human psyche. It's like asking somebody to swallow the sun.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這只是在人類脆弱的靈魂上放上太多的責任 這就像要一個人去吞下太陽

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: It just completely warps and distorts egos, and it creates all these unmanageable expectations about performance. And I think the pressure of that
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這只會完全扭曲挫敗一個人的自我 並製造出無法預期與掌握的成果 我想這帶來的壓力

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: has been killing off our artists for the last 500 years. And, if this is true, and I think it is true, the question becomes, what now? Can we do this differently?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 已在過去的五百年中扼殺了我們身旁的藝術家 而，如果這是真的 我想這也的確是真的 現在面臨到的問題是，怎麻辦？ 我們是否可以有不同的作為？

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Maybe go back to some more ancient understanding about the relationship between humans and the creative mystery. Maybe not. Maybe we can't just erase 500 years of rational humanistic thought
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 也許可以回溯到更古老的理解方式 去看待人們與創造奧祕間的關係 也許不行 也許我們不能就這樣在十八分鐘的演講中

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: in one 18 minute speech. And there's probably people in this audience who would raise really legitimate scientific suspicions about the notion of, basically, fairies
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 就這樣抹煞了五百多年的理性人文主義 也許在今天的觀眾中 會有人提出相當合法的科學懷疑 懷疑有關精靈的這種基本觀念

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: who follow people around rubbing fairy juice on their projects and stuff. I'm not, probably, going to bring you all along with me on this. But the question that I kind of want to pose is --
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這些精靈跟著人們到處跑並在作品上撒下精靈水 或許，我並無法讓你們同意我的看法 但我要提出的問題是

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: you know, why not? Why not think about it this way? Because it makes as much sense as anything else I have ever heard in terms of explaining the utter maddening capriciousness of the creative process.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你知道的，為何不這樣想？ 為何不用這樣的方式作思考呢？ 因為這就跟我聽過的其他事情一樣地有道理 尤其是在解釋這徹底瘋狂並變化無常的 創作過程

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: A process which, as anybody who has ever tried to make something -- which is to say basically everyone here --- knows does not always behave rationally.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 在創作過程中，當一個人試著創作出些什麼時  都知道創作者並不總是理性地創作著

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And, in fact, can sometimes feel downright paranormal. I had this encounter recently where I met the extraordinary American poet Ruth Stone, who's now in her 90s, but she's been a poet her entire life
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 而且，事實上，我們有時會覺得完全超乎常理 當我最近遇到傑出的美國詩人露絲·斯通時，就有這樣的感受  她現在約九十多歲，在其一生中，她一直扮演詩人的角色

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and she told me that when she was growing up in rural Virginia, she would be out working in the fields, and she said she would feel and hear a poem coming at her from over the landscape.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 她告訴我她成長於維吉利亞的鄉間 她會在外面的田野工作著  從山水景色中來到她眼前

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And she said it was like a thunderous train of air. And it would come barreling down at her over the landscape. And she felt it coming, because it would shake the earth under her feet.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 她說這就像是列車行駛下轟隆轟隆響的空氣 在山水之間向她傾洩而下

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: She knew that she had only one thing to do at that point, and that was to, in her words, "run like hell." And she would run like hell to the house and she would be getting chased by this poem,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 她知道此時她只有一件事要做，用她的話來說，那就是「拼命奔跑」。她會拼命跑回家，然後被這首詩追趕，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and the whole deal was that she had to get to a piece of paper and a pencil fast enough so that when it thundered through her, she could collect it and grab it on the page.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 整個過程是，她必須足夠快地拿到一張紙和一支鉛筆，以便當它轟鳴著穿過她時，她可以收集它並抓住它在紙上。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And other times she wouldn't be fast enough, so she'd be running and running, and she wouldn't get to the house and the poem would barrel through her and she would miss it
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 有時她跑得不夠快，所以她會跑啊跑，但她無法到達房子，這首詩就會從她身上掠過，她就會錯過它。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and she said it would continue on across the landscape, looking, as she put it "for another poet." And then there were these times -- this is the piece I never forgot --
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 她說它將繼續穿越這片土地，尋找，正如她所說的「另一位詩人」。然後有一些時候——這是我永遠不會忘記的片段——

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: she said that there were moments where she would almost miss it, right? So, she's running to the house and she's looking for the paper and the poem passes through her,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 她說有些時刻她幾乎會錯過，對嗎？於是，她跑向房子，尋找那張紙，這首詩從她的腦海中閃過，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and she grabs a pencil just as it's going through her, and then she said, it was like she would reach out with her other hand and she would catch it. She would catch the poem by its tail,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當一支鉛筆穿過她時，她抓住了它，然後她說，就像她伸出另一隻手，她就會抓住它。她會抓住這首詩的尾巴，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and she would pull it backwards into her body as she was transcribing on the page. And in these instances, the poem would come up on the page perfect and intact
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 並同時謄寫於紙本上時 就在這些瞬間中，詩會精準完美地躍然紙上

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: but backwards, from the last word to the first. (Laughter) So when I heard that I was like -- that's uncanny, that's exactly what my creative process is like. (Laughter)
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 由後往前，從最後一個字到第一個字 （笑聲） 所以當我聽到我就像--這真的很不可思議 這簡直像是的我創作過程 （笑聲）

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: That's not at all what my creative process is -- I'm not the pipeline! I'm a mule, and the way that I have to work is I have to get up at the same time every day,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這也不完全就是我的創作過程，我並不像一個輸油管

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and sweat and labor and barrel through it really awkwardly. But even I, in my mulishness, even I have brushed up against that thing, at times. And I would imagine that a lot of you have too.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 即使我不時潤飾我的作品 我可以想像得到你們大多數人也會這樣

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: You know, even I have had work or ideas come through me from a source that I honestly cannot identify. And what is that thing?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 老實說，我無法認清這源頭 而這是什麼呢？

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And how are we to relate to it in a way that will not make us lose our minds, but, in fact, might actually keep us sane? And for me, the best contemporary example that I have of how to do that
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我們要用怎樣的方式與其相處卻不會讓我們喪失心智 而是，事實上，卻很有可能讓我們保持清醒呢？ 對我來說，當代有個最好的例子讓我知道怎樣做到

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: is the musician Tom Waits, who I got to interview several years ago on a magazine assignment. And we were talking about this, and you know, Tom, for most of his life, he was pretty much the embodiment
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 那就是音樂家湯姆威茲 我幾年前曾因雜誌社的工作需要去採訪過他 我們討論著這個話題

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: of the tormented contemporary modern artist, trying to control and manage and dominate these sort of uncontrollable creative impulses that were totally internalized.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這些無法掌控的創造衝動 而這些衝動完全是內化的

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: But then he got older, he got calmer, and one day he was driving down the freeway in Los Angeles, and this is when it all changed for him. And he's speeding along, and all of a sudden
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 但隨著年紀增長，他也越趨內斂 有天他自洛杉磯的高速公路開車，他告訴我 而這就是改變他一生的時刻

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: he hears this little fragment of melody, that comes into his head as inspiration often comes, elusive and tantalizing, and he wants it, it's gorgeous, and he longs for it, but he has no way to get it.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這就像靈感臨到他腦海中，難以捉摸卻又令人心動著急 你知道的，他迫切地想抓住它 這是如此的珍貴，而他迫切地渴望著，但他沒辦法得著

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: He doesn't have a piece of paper, or a pencil, or a tape recorder. So he starts to feel all of that old anxiety start to rise in him like, "I'm going to lose this thing,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 他沒有一張紙，一支鉛筆，或是一台錄音機。所以他開始感覺到所有舊有的焦慮開始在他體內升起，就像，「我要失去這個東西了，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and I'll be be haunted by this song forever. I'm not good enough, and I can't do it." And instead of panicking, he just stopped.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: "然後這旋律又要在我腦海中永遠揮之不去！"

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: He just stopped that whole mental process and he did something completely novel. He just looked up at the sky, and he said, "Excuse me, can you not see that I'm driving?" (Laughter)
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 他只是停下這所有的心理過程  "不好意思，難道你看不出來我正在開車嗎？" (笑聲）

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: "Do I look like I can write down a song right now? If you really want to exist, come back at a more opportune moment when I can take care of you. Otherwise, go bother somebody else today.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: "我現在看起來像是可以寫下一首歌嗎？ 如果你真的想要給我這樣的靈感，那就等等適合的時候 等我有空應付的時候再來 否則，今天就先去找別人吧

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Go bother Leonard Cohen." And his whole work process changed after that. Not the work, the work was still oftentimes as dark as ever. But the process, and the heavy anxiety around it
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 去麻煩李歐納科恩吧 接著他整個的創作過程都改變了 並不是作品本身，他的作品仍像以往一般黑暗 但這過程，還有這其中巨大的焦慮感

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: was released when he took the genie, the genius out of him where it was causing nothing but trouble, and released it back where it came from,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 都在他將這創作的精靈解放出來時，也獲得舒緩

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and realized that this didn't have to be this internalized, tormented thing. It could be this peculiar, wondrous, bizarre collaboration, kind of conversation between Tom and the strange, external thing
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 有點像是湯姆和不太像湯姆本身

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: that was not quite Tom. When I heard that story, it started to shift a little bit the way that I worked too, and this idea already saved me once.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這奇異外界事物間的對話

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: It saved me when I was in the middle of writing "Eat, Pray, Love," and I fell into one of those sort of pits of despair that we all fall into when we're working on something and it's not coming
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當我們在專注創作於某個作品時，我們會陷進去，靈感卻一直不來¥¥

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and you start to think this is going to be a disaster, the worst book ever written. Not just bad, but the worst book ever written. And I started to think I should just dump this project.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你開始認為這將是一場災難，是有史以來最糟糕的書。不僅糟糕，而且是有史以來最糟糕的書。我開始想我應該放棄這個計畫。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: But then I remembered Tom talking to the open air and I tried it. So I just lifted my face up from the manuscript and I directed my comments to an empty corner of the room.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 但接著我又想到湯姆和空氣喊話的場景 我試了一試 所以我就從手稿中抬起頭來 我對著房間的空角落講出了心中的想法

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And I said aloud, "Listen you, thing, you and I both know that if this book isn't brilliant that is not entirely my fault, right? Because you can see that I am putting everything I have into this,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 接著我大聲地喊著，"你！聽著！ 你跟我都知道如果這本書不夠精彩 這並不完全是我的錯，對嗎？ 因為你看的出來我已經盡全力去創作了

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: I don't have any more than this. If you want it to be better, you've got to show up and do your part of the deal. But if you don't do that, you know what, the hell with it.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我沒有比這個更多的了。如果你想讓事情變得更好，你就必須出現並盡自己的一份力量。但如果你不這樣做，你知道嗎，那就見鬼去吧。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: I'm going to keep writing anyway because that's my job. And I would please like the record to reflect today that I showed up for my part of the job." (Laughter) Because -- (Applause)
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因為 （掌聲）

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Because in the end it's like this, OK -- centuries ago in the deserts of North Africa, people used to gather for these moonlight dances of sacred dance and music
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 到最後會有點像 在幾世紀前的北非沙漠中 人們會為著月光下神聖的舞蹈與音樂聚集著

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: that would go on for hours and hours, until dawn. They were always magnificent, because the dancers were professionals and they were terrific, right?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這會延續著數小時，直到黎明 這表演總是十分壯觀，因為舞者門相當的專業

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: But every once in a while, very rarely, something would happen, and one of these performers would actually become transcendent. And I know you know what I'm talking about,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 其中會有一位表演者真的表現得比別人優越 我知道你們都明白我在講什麼

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: because I know you've all seen, at some point in your life, a performance like this. It was like time would stop, and the dancer would sort of step through some kind of portal
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因為我知道在你們生命中某些時刻一定看過像這樣的表演   舞者會有點像是穿越過某種入口

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and he wasn't doing anything different than he had ever done, 1,000 nights before, but everything would align. And all of a sudden, he would no longer appear to be merely human.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 但一切都會安排好 突然之間，他表現得不再像只是一個人類

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: He would be lit from within, and lit from below and all lit up on fire with divinity. And when this happened, back then, people knew it for what it was, you know, they called it by its name.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因著這神靈，所有的事物都跟著在火上發光 當這樣的事發生時 當時的人們都知道為何會這樣，你知道的，他們會呼喊它的名

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: They would put their hands together and they would start to chant, "Allah, Allah, Allah, God, God, God." That's God, you know. Curious historical footnote:
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 他們會把手牽起來，開始吟唱著  這就是神，你知道嗎？ 這是很奇異的歷史註腳

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: when the Moors invaded southern Spain, they took this custom with them and the pronunciation changed over the centuries from "Allah, Allah, Allah," to "Olé, olé, olé,"
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當摩爾帝國侵略南西班牙時，他們帶著這個習俗 而在過去的幾個世紀裡，發音也隨著改變

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: which you still hear in bullfights and in flamenco dances. In Spain, when a performer has done something impossible and magic, "Allah, olé, olé, Allah, magnificent, bravo,"
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這你可能在鬥牛賽與佛朗明歌中聽到 在西班牙，當一個表演者做了件不可能且不可思議的表演時 "阿拉！歐勒！歐勒！阿拉！棒呆了！好極了！

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: incomprehensible, there it is -- a glimpse of God. Which is great, because we need that. But, the tricky bit comes the next morning, for the dancer himself, when he wakes up and discovers
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 不可置信的，就在那裡，你在表演者身上看到上天的化身  但，隔天早上詭譎的事發生了 當舞者他自己起床時

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: that it's Tuesday at 11 a.m., and he's no longer a glimpse of God. He's just an aging mortal with really bad knees, and maybe he's never going to ascend to that height again.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 他只會個會老化的人類，並有著受傷的膝蓋 也許他再也無法到達那樣的表演高峰

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And maybe nobody will ever chant God's name again as he spins, and what is he then to do with the rest of his life? This is hard. This is one of the most painful reconciliations to make
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 也許當他表演旋轉時，再也沒有人會吟唱著神的名字 那他餘生該怎麼辦呢？ 這相當棘手 這是我們在創作生涯中

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: in a creative life. But maybe it doesn't have to be quite so full of anguish if you never happened to believe, in the first place, that the most extraordinary aspects of your being came from you.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 必須做的最痛苦的妥協之一 但也許這一切不用這樣的痛苦 如果你在一開始就不曾相信 你自我最獨特的部分是來自於你本身

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: But maybe if you just believed that they were on loan to you from some unimaginable source for some exquisite portion of your life to be passed along when you're finished, with somebody else.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 也許你只是深信這些不凡的能力是借給你的 來自某個不可思議的地方，為著成就你生命中某個細膩的部分

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And, you know, if we think about it this way, it starts to change everything. This is how I've started to think, and this is certainly how I've been thinking in the last few months
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這當然也是過去幾個月我所思考的方式

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: as I've been working on the book that will soon be published, as the dangerously, frighteningly over-anticipated follow up to my freakish success. And what I have to sort of keep telling myself
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 同時我也忙於創作即將出版的下一本書  上一本熱門暢銷書的續集 我所必須一直告訴我自己的是

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: when I get really psyched out about that is don't be afraid. Don't be daunted. Just do your job. Continue to show up for your piece of it, whatever that might be.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當我因創作又真的快精神崩潰時 別畏懼 繼續堅守在你的工作崗位上，無論這工作是什麼

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: If your job is to dance, do your dance. If the divine, cockeyed genius assigned to your case decides to let some sort of wonderment be glimpsed, for just one moment through your efforts, then "Olé!"
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 如果那個分派到你創作上的那個非凡卻又荒誕的精靈 決定在某些瞬間讓令人讚嘆的事 透過你的創作被看見，那就真的太棒了

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And if not, do your dance anyhow. And "Olé!" to you, nonetheless. I believe this and I feel that we must teach it. "Olé!" to you, nonetheless, just for having the sheer human love and stubbornness
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 即使如此，你還是最棒的 我深信不疑，也覺得我們應該把這個觀念教導出去  憑藉著人類單純的愛與執著

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: to keep showing up. Thank you. (Applause) Thank you. (Applause) June Cohen: Olé! (Applause)
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 持續地堅守著我們的工作崗位 謝謝 （掌聲） 謝謝大家 （掌聲） 瓊恩科恩：歐勒 （掌聲）

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I'm a storyteller. And I would like to tell you a few personal stories about what I like to call "the danger of the single story." I grew up on a university campus in eastern Nigeria.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我是一位作家。 今天想向各位分享我的幾個故事 以及一個我稱做是「單一故事的危險性」。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: My mother says that I started reading at the age of two, although I think four is probably close to the truth. So I was an early reader, and what I read were British and American children's books.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我母親說我兩歲就開始讀書，儘管我認為四歲可能更接近事實。所以我是一個很早的讀者，讀的都是英國和美國的兒童讀物。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I was also an early writer, and when I began to write, at about the age of seven, stories in pencil with crayon illustrations that my poor mother was obligated to read,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 大約七歲就開始寫故事， 用鉛筆寫故事加上蠟筆畫的插圖，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I wrote exactly the kinds of stories I was reading: All my characters were white and blue-eyed, they played in the snow, they ate apples, (Laughter) and they talked a lot about the weather,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 他們在雪中玩耍

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: how lovely it was that the sun had come out. (Laughter) Now, this despite the fact that I lived in Nigeria. I had never been outside Nigeria. We didn't have snow, we ate mangoes,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 太陽出來了，真是太可愛了。 （笑聲） 現在，儘管我住在尼日利亞。我從未出過尼日利亞。我們沒有下雪，我們吃的是芒果，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and we never talked about the weather, because there was no need to. My characters also drank a lot of ginger beer, because the characters in the British books I read drank ginger beer.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 因為那些英美童書中的角色

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Never mind that I had no idea what ginger beer was. (Laughter) And for many years afterwards, I would have a desperate desire to taste ginger beer. But that is another story.
What this demonstrates, I think, is how impressionable and vulnerable we are in the face of a story, particularly as children. Because all I had read were books in which characters were foreign,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 從我個人的經驗，我想   尤其是小孩子。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I had become convinced that books by their very nature had to have foreigners in them and had to be about things with which I could not personally identify.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我開始相信，書籍就其本質而言必然含有外國人，並且必定涉及我個人無法認同的事物。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Now, things changed when I discovered African books. There weren't many of them available, and they weren't quite as easy to find as the foreign books.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 當時這樣的作品並不多 也不像那些外國書容易取得。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: But because of writers like Chinua Achebe and Camara Laye, I went through a mental shift in my perception of literature. I realized that people like me, girls with skin the color of chocolate,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我對文學作品的看法 我了解到，像我這樣的人：

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: whose kinky hair could not form ponytails, could also exist in literature. I started to write about things I recognized. Now, I loved those American and British books I read.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 捲曲的頭髮無法紮成馬尾辮，也可能存在於文學中。我開始寫我認識的事。現在，我喜歡讀過的美國和英國書籍。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: They stirred my imagination. They opened up new worlds for me. But the unintended consequence was that I did not know that people like me could exist in literature.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 它們激發了我的想像力、為我開啟新的世界。 但這種結果是 我認為像我這樣的人

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: So what the discovery of African writers did for me was this: It saved me from having a single story of what books are. I come from a conventional, middle-class Nigerian family.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 讓我對於文學 我們家是普通的中產階級

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: My father was a professor. My mother was an administrator. And so we had, as was the norm, live-in domestic help, who would often come from nearby rural villages.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我的父親是教授

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: So, the year I turned eight, we got a new house boy. His name was Fide. The only thing my mother told us about him was that his family was very poor.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 所以，我八歲那年，我們有了一個新的男僕。他的名字叫菲德。關於他，我母親唯一告訴我們的是，他的家庭非常貧窮。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: My mother sent yams and rice, and our old clothes, to his family. And when I didn't finish my dinner, my mother would say, "Finish your food! Don't you know? People like Fide's family have nothing."
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我媽媽會送蕃薯、米 如果我晚餐沒吃完，我媽會說

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: So I felt enormous pity for Fide's family. Then one Saturday, we went to his village to visit, and his mother showed us a beautifully patterned basket made of dyed raffia that his brother had made.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 所以我非常可憐 Fide。   是他哥哥用染色的棕櫚樹葉編成的

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I was startled. It had not occurred to me that anybody in his family could actually make something. All I had heard about them was how poor they were,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我嚇傻了。 我從沒想過他們家的人 有能力作出那樣的東西 我所聽到的只有他們多窮

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: so that it had become impossible for me to see them as anything else but poor. Their poverty was my single story of them. Years later, I thought about this when I left Nigeria
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 所以我眼中的他們，除了窮之外  幾年後，我到美國唸大學

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: to go to university in the United States. I was 19. My American roommate was shocked by me. She asked where I had learned to speak English so well, and was confused when I said that Nigeria
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 她問我去哪學這麼標準的英文

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: happened to have English as its official language. She asked if she could listen to what she called my "tribal music," and was consequently very disappointed when I produced my tape of Mariah Carey.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 結果看到我拿出瑪麗亞凱莉的時候 整個大感失望。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: (Laughter) She assumed that I did not know how to use a stove. What struck me was this: She had felt sorry for me even before she saw me. Her default position toward me, as an African,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: (笑) 她想當然地認為

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: was a kind of patronizing, well-meaning pity. My roommate had a single story of Africa: a single story of catastrophe. In this single story,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 是可憐、好意的憐憫 我室友對非洲有個單一故事

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: there was no possibility of Africans being similar to her in any way, no possibility of feelings more complex than pity, no possibility of a connection as human equals.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 容不下除了憐憫之外的態度 容不下同是人類則生而平等。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I must say that before I went to the U.S., I didn't consciously identify as African. But in the U.S., whenever Africa came up, people turned to me.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我承認我到美國之前  但在美國，只要提到「非洲」，大家就會轉向我

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Never mind that I knew nothing about places like Namibia. But I did come to embrace this new identity, and in many ways I think of myself now as African.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 也不管我對像納米比亞的地方一點都不了解。 但我雙手擁抱這個新身分

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Although I still get quite irritable when Africa is referred to as a country, the most recent example being my otherwise wonderful flight from Lagos two days ago,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 但聽到大家把非洲當成一個國家時 最近一次，就是在兩天前拉哥斯起飛的班機上 這趟旅行近乎完美

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: in which there was an announcement on the Virgin flight about the charity work in "India, Africa and other countries." (Laughter) So, after I had spent some years in the U.S. as an African,
I began to understand my roommate's response to me. If I had not grown up in Nigeria, and if all I knew about Africa were from popular images,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我漸漸了解我室友的反應。 如果我不是在奈及利亞長大，我所認識的非洲

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I too would think that Africa was a place of beautiful landscapes, beautiful animals, and incomprehensible people, fighting senseless wars, dying of poverty and AIDS, unable to speak for themselves
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 和野蠻人 打著沒意義的仗、死於貧窮與愛滋

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and waiting to be saved by a kind, white foreigner. I would see Africans in the same way that I, as a child, had seen Fide's family.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 等待好心的白人

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: This single story of Africa ultimately comes, I think, from Western literature. Now, here is a quote from the writing of a London merchant called John Lok, who sailed to west Africa in 1561
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 這種對於非洲的單一故事，我想是從西方文學開始的。 下面是一位英國商人 他在1561年航行到西非

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and kept a fascinating account of his voyage. After referring to the black Africans as "beasts who have no houses," he writes, "They are also people without heads,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 並且詳細的記下他的航程。  他寫道: 「他們沒有頭」

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: having their mouth and eyes in their breasts." Now, I've laughed every time I've read this. And one must admire the imagination of John Lok. But what is important about his writing
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 「嘴巴和眼睛長在胸部」 我每次讀完每次笑。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: is that it represents the beginning of a tradition of telling African stories in the West: A tradition of Sub-Saharan Africa as a place of negatives, of difference, of darkness,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 西方人眼中傳統的非洲印象 一種次撒哈拉非洲的負面印象

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: of people who, in the words of the wonderful poet Rudyard Kipling, are "half devil, half child." And so, I began to realize that my American roommate must have throughout her life
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 「半是惡魔，半是人」  她的一生

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: seen and heard different versions of this single story, as had a professor, who once told me that my novel was not "authentically African." Now, I was quite willing to contend
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 一定聽過各種版本的 就如有位教授 曾告訴我，我的小說描寫的不是「真正的非洲」。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that there were a number of things wrong with the novel, that it had failed in a number of places, but I had not quite imagined that it had failed at achieving something called African authenticity.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 但我很難想像我的小說

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: In fact, I did not know what African authenticity was. The professor told me that my characters were too much like him, an educated and middle-class man. My characters drove cars.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 而事實上我不曉得 那位教授說，我書中的角色

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: They were not starving. Therefore they were not authentically African. But I must quickly add that I too am just as guilty in the question of the single story.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 所以他們不是真正的非洲人。 但我也要馬上承認我自己 對別人也犯過單一故事的錯。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: A few years ago, I visited Mexico from the U.S. The political climate in the U.S. at the time was tense, and there were debates going on about immigration. And, as often happens in America,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 幾年前，我到墨西哥 當時美國的政治情況有點緊張  在美國就會常常聽到

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: immigration became synonymous with Mexicans. There were endless stories of Mexicans as people who were fleecing the healthcare system, sneaking across the border,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 移民等同於墨西哥人這一類的話。   從邊境溜進來

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: being arrested at the border, that sort of thing. I remember walking around on my first day in Guadalajara, watching the people going to work, rolling up tortillas in the marketplace,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 在邊界被逮捕之類的事。  看著工作的人們， 市場裡有人做西班牙蛋餅

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: smoking, laughing. I remember first feeling slight surprise. And then, I was overwhelmed with shame. I realized that I had been so immersed in the media coverage of Mexicans
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 抽菸、大笑。  隨後感到非常丟臉

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that they had become one thing in my mind, the abject immigrant. I had bought into the single story of Mexicans and I could not have been more ashamed of myself.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 以至於他們在我心中的形象

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: So that is how to create a single story, show a people as one thing, as only one thing, over and over again, and that is what they become. It is impossible to talk about the single story
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 描述同一種人 一遍又一遍， 最後他們就會變成那樣。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: without talking about power. There is a word, an Igbo word, that I think about whenever I think about the power structures of the world, and it is "nkali."
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 不談權力。每當我想到世界的權力結構時，我就會想到一個詞，一個伊博語詞，它就是「nkali」。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: It's a noun that loosely translates to "to be greater than another." Like our economic and political worlds, stories too are defined by the principle of nkali: How they are told, who tells them,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 故事如何傳遞、誰來傳遞，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: when they're told, how many stories are told, are really dependent on power. Power is the ability not just to tell the story of another person, but to make it the definitive story of that person.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 什麼時候、多少次  權力不只能述說故事

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: The Palestinian poet Mourid Barghouti writes that if you want to dispossess a people, the simplest way to do it is to tell their story and to start with, "secondly."
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 巴勒斯坦詩人穆里‧巴爾古提曾說  最簡單的方法就是說故事 而且從「第二點」開頭。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Start the story with the arrows of the Native Americans, and not with the arrival of the British, and you have an entirely different story. Start the story with the failure of the African state,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 就會有全然不同的故事。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and not with the colonial creation of the African state, and you have an entirely different story. I recently spoke at a university where a student told me that it was such a shame
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 就會有全然不同的故事。 我最近到一所大學演講 有個學生告訴我

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that Nigerian men were physical abusers like the father character in my novel. I told him that I had just read a novel called "American Psycho" -- (Laughter) -- and that it was such a shame
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我告訴他，我最近看了一本小說 (笑) 真是可恥

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that young Americans were serial murderers. (Laughter) (Applause) Now, obviously I said this in a fit of mild irritation. (Laughter) But it would never have occurred to me to think
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 美國年輕人都是殺人魔 (笑) (掌聲) 當時我實在有點不悅 (笑) 我從沒有因為

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that just because I had read a novel in which a character was a serial killer that he was somehow representative of all Americans. This is not because I am a better person than that student,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我就認為所有美國人 當然不是因為我比那個學生好

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: but because of America's cultural and economic power, I had many stories of America. I had read Tyler and Updike and Steinbeck and Gaitskill. I did not have a single story of America.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 而是因為美國的文化、經濟地位 所以我對美國有多重故事 我讀Tyler、Updike、Steinbeck、Gaitskill的書 對美國，我沒有單一故事。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: When I learned, some years ago, that writers were expected to have had really unhappy childhoods to be successful, I began to think about how I could invent horrible things my parents had done to me.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我就開始想要編一些我父母

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: (Laughter) But the truth is that I had a very happy childhood, full of laughter and love, in a very close-knit family. But I also had grandfathers who died in refugee camps.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 但事實是，我有個快樂的童年 充滿歡笑和愛，家人很親近 但同時，我祖父死在難民營

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: My cousin Polle died because he could not get adequate healthcare. One of my closest friends, Okoloma, died in a plane crash because our fire trucks did not have water.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我最好的朋友Okoloma死於墜機 因為消防車上沒有水可以救火。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I grew up under repressive military governments that devalued education, so that sometimes, my parents were not paid their salaries. And so, as a child, I saw jam disappear from the breakfast table,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我生活在高壓統治 政府不重視教育 我父母有時是領不到薪水的。 所以在小時候，我看著早餐的果醬消失

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: then margarine disappeared, then bread became too expensive, then milk became rationed. And most of all, a kind of normalized political fear invaded our lives. All of these stories make me who I am.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 然後牛奶定額配給 而最嚴重的是政治恐懼

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: But to insist on only these negative stories is to flatten my experience and to overlook the many other stories that formed me. The single story creates stereotypes,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 就簡化了我個人的生活經歷， 也忽略了同樣造就我

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and the problem with stereotypes is not that they are untrue, but that they are incomplete. They make one story become the only story. Of course, Africa is a continent full of catastrophes:
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 而刻板印象的問題就是 而是不完整  當然，非洲充滿苦難

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: There are immense ones, such as the horrific rapes in Congo and depressing ones, such as the fact that 5,000 people apply for one job vacancy in Nigeria.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 有很悲傷的，像是

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: But there are other stories that are not about catastrophe, and it is very important, it is just as important, to talk about them. I've always felt that it is impossible
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 但也有其他美好的故事  我總覺得要完全的了解

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: to engage properly with a place or a person without engaging with all of the stories of that place and that person. The consequence of the single story is this: It robs people of dignity.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 一個地方或是一個人 不去了解全部的故事，是不可能的。 述說單一故事的後果是

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: It makes our recognition of our equal humanity difficult. It emphasizes how we are different rather than how we are similar. So what if before my Mexican trip,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 只強調我們有多麼不同 如果我去墨西哥旅行前

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I had followed the immigration debate from both sides, the U.S. and the Mexican? What if my mother had told us that Fide's family was poor and hardworking? What if we had an African television network
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 事情會變的如何？ 如果我媽媽告訴我們Fide家雖窮 卻很努力工作？ 如果有家非洲電視台

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that broadcast diverse African stories all over the world? What the Nigerian writer Chinua Achebe calls "a balance of stories." What if my roommate knew about my Nigerian publisher, Muhtar Bakare,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 奈及利亞作家Chinua Achebe稱之為

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: a remarkable man who left his job in a bank to follow his dream and start a publishing house? Now, the conventional wisdom was that Nigerians don't read literature. He disagreed.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 他決然的離開銀行的工作

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: He felt that people who could read, would read, if you made literature affordable and available to them. Shortly after he published my first novel, I went to a TV station in Lagos to do an interview,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我到拉哥斯一家電視台接受採訪

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and a woman who worked there as a messenger came up to me and said, "I really liked your novel. I didn't like the ending. Now, you must write a sequel, and this is what will happen ..." (Laughter)
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 一個工作人員走上來告訴我  「你一定要寫續集，然後要這樣這樣寫...」

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: And she went on to tell me what to write in the sequel. I was not only charmed, I was very moved. Here was a woman, part of the ordinary masses of Nigerians, who were not supposed to be readers.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 一位普通的奈及利亞女人 照理說不會看書

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: She had not only read the book, but she had taken ownership of it and felt justified in telling me what to write in the sequel. Now, what if my roommate knew about my friend Funmi Iyanda,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 她不僅讀過這本書，而且已經擁有了這本書，並覺得有理由告訴我在續集中要寫什麼。現在，如果我的室友知道我的朋友 Funmi Iyanda 會怎麼樣？

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: a fearless woman who hosts a TV show in Lagos, and is determined to tell the stories that we prefer to forget? What if my roommate knew about the heart procedure
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 勇敢的拉哥斯電視台主持人 決定要述說人們寧可遺忘的故事

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that was performed in the Lagos hospital last week? What if my roommate knew about contemporary Nigerian music, talented people singing in English and Pidgin, and Igbo and Yoruba and Ijo,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 的一個心臟手術，會如何呢？ 如果我室友知道當代奈及利亞音樂 是融合各種語言的美妙樂曲，英語、皮欽語 伊博語、約魯巴語、伊喬語

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: mixing influences from Jay-Z to Fela to Bob Marley to their grandfathers. What if my roommate knew about the female lawyer who recently went to court in Nigeria to challenge a ridiculous law
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 帶點Jay-Z和菲拉庫堤的曲風 從Bob Marley到他們的祖父 如果我室友聽過一位女律師

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that required women to get their husband's consent before renewing their passports? What if my roommate knew about Nollywood, full of innovative people making films despite great technical odds,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 規定女人要更新護照 需要丈夫同意，會如何呢？ 如果我室友知道奈萊塢 創意的人們利用有限的技術拍攝電影，會如何呢？

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: films so popular that they really are the best example of Nigerians consuming what they produce? What if my roommate knew about my wonderfully ambitious hair braider,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 正是奈及利亞人自給自足 如果我室友認識我的編髮師

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: who has just started her own business selling hair extensions? Or about the millions of other Nigerians who start businesses and sometimes fail, but continue to nurse ambition?
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 或是聽說過奈及利亞 還是不放棄的故事？

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Every time I am home I am confronted with the usual sources of irritation for most Nigerians: our failed infrastructure, our failed government, but also by the incredible resilience
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我每次回家都會面對 多數奈及利亞人感到不悅的事情 失敗的基礎建設、失敗的政府

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: of people who thrive despite the government, rather than because of it. I teach writing workshops in Lagos every summer, and it is amazing to me how many people apply,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 而不是氣餒  看到那麼多申請我感到很驚訝

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: how many people are eager to write, to tell stories. My Nigerian publisher and I have just started a non-profit called Farafina Trust, and we have big dreams of building libraries
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 有這麼多人急著想要 寫出他們的故事。   我們的夢想是建圖書館

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and refurbishing libraries that already exist and providing books for state schools that don't have anything in their libraries, and also of organizing lots and lots of workshops,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 整修現有的圖書館  因為他們圖書館裡沒有書籍

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: in reading and writing, for all the people who are eager to tell our many stories. Stories matter. Many stories matter. Stories have been used to dispossess and to malign,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 讓人們說出自己的故事。  多元的故事很重要。 有些故事被用來醜化現實

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: but stories can also be used to empower and to humanize. Stories can break the dignity of a people, but stories can also repair that broken dignity. The American writer Alice Walker wrote this
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 但故事也可以用來激勵強化人道精神，  但有些故事能讓人重拾尊嚴。 美國作家愛麗絲渥克寫了

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: about her Southern relatives who had moved to the North. She introduced them to a book about the Southern life that they had left behind. "They sat around, reading the book themselves,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 她介紹一本書給他們 內容有關他們所拋下的南方生活

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: listening to me read the book, and a kind of paradise was regained." I would like to end with this thought: That when we reject the single story, when we realize that there is never a single story
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 「邊聽我說故事，並重拾了心中的樂園。」   當我們了解，世上沒有任何地方

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: about any place, we regain a kind of paradise. Thank you. (Applause)
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 只有單一個故事時

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Seven years ago, a student came to me and asked me to invest in his company. He said, "I'm working with three friends, and we're going to try to disrupt an industry by selling stuff online."
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 七年前，有一個學生來找我， 想要我投資他的公司。 他說，「我正在和三個朋友合作，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: And I said, "OK, you guys spent the whole summer on this, right?" "No, we all took internships just in case it doesn't work out." "All right, but you're going to go in full time once you graduate."
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 然後我說，「 好啊，你們一整個夏天 都在搞這件事？，對吧？」 「 不，我們也在實習， 預防失敗後也有個後路」 「 好吧，但是你們畢業之後， 要全身心地投入到這個工作之中」。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: "Not exactly. We've all lined up backup jobs." Six months go by, it's the day before the company launches, and there is still not a functioning website.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 「 也不全是，我們都安排好了備份的工作」。  已經到了公司開業的前一天， 但他們仍沒有一個可以用的網站。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: "You guys realize, the entire company is a website. That's literally all it is." So I obviously declined to invest. And they ended up naming the company Warby Parker. (Laughter)
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 「 你們要知道，整個公司就是一個網站。 實際上，這就是它的全部」。 所以，我直接拒絕投資。 他們最終給公司取了個名字， 叫 Warby Parke （眼鏡電商）。 （笑聲）

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: They sell glasses online. They were recently recognized as the world's most innovative company and valued at over a billion dollars. And now? My wife handles our investments. Why was I so wrong?
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 他們在線上賣眼鏡。 最近，他們被認為是世界上 最有創新力的公司，  然後現在呢？換我的老婆在管錢了。 為什麼我錯的這麼離譜？

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: To find out, I've been studying people that I come to call "originals." Originals are nonconformists, people who not only have new ideas but take action to champion them.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 為了找出答案，我一直在研究一群人， 我稱他們為「原創者」。 原創者是那些不按常理行事的人， 那些人不僅有新穎的點子， 並且付出行動去挑戰它們。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: They are people who stand out and speak up. Originals drive creativity and change in the world. They're the people you want to bet on. And they look nothing like I expected.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 這些人敢於站出表達自己的想法。 原創者駕馭著世上的創造力和改變。 這是一群你想要為之下注的人。 但是他們看上去並不是你期望的人。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I want to show you today three things I've learned about recognizing originals and becoming a little bit more like them. So the first reason that I passed on Warby Parker
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我今天想要告訴各位， 我學到的三件事， 有關於如何辯識原創者， 以及如何變得更像他們。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: was they were really slow getting off the ground. Now, you are all intimately familiar with the mind of a procrastinator. Well, I have a confession for you. I'm the opposite. I'm a precrastinator.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 有件事我必須向你們承認，我剛好相反， 我是個「提前症患者」。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Yes, that's an actual term. You know that panic you feel a few hours before a big deadline when you haven't done anything yet. I just feel that a few months ahead of time. (Laughter)
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 而我卻是覺得我提前了幾個月。 (笑聲)

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: So this started early: when I was a kid, I took Nintendo games very seriously. I would wake up at 5am, start playing and not stop until I had mastered them.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 很早前我就是這樣：我小時候， 非常癡迷於任天堂的遊戲。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Eventually it got so out of hand that a local newspaper came and did a story on the dark side of Nintendo, starring me. (Laughter) (Applause) Since then, I have traded hair for teeth. (Laughter)
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: (笑聲) (掌聲) 從此之後，我就開始 用我的頭髮去換牙齒了。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: But this served me well in college, because I finished my senior thesis four months before the deadline. And I was proud of that, until a few years ago.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但這讓我在大學時從中受益， 因為我在截止期前四個月 就完成了我的畢業論文。 我非常的驕傲，直到幾年前，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I had a student named Jihae, who came to me and said, "I have my most creative ideas when I'm procrastinating." And I was like, "That's cute, where are the four papers you owe me?" (Laughter)
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我有一個叫做 Jihae 的學生， 找到我對我說： 「在我拖延的時候， 我的創造力總是噴湧而出」 然後我說：「你真可愛， 那你欠我的四篇論文呢？」 （笑聲）

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: No, she was one of our most creative students, and as an organizational psychologist, this is the kind of idea that I test. So I challenged her to get some data. She goes into a bunch of companies.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 不，她是我們最有創造力的學生之一，  所以，我質問她，請她拿數據出來。 她去了很多家公司。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: She has people fill out surveys about how often they procrastinate. Then she gets their bosses to rate how creative and innovative they are. And sure enough, the precrastinators like me,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 她讓人們填寫了關於 他們拖延頻率的調查問卷。 她找到他們的上司， 去給他們的創新力和創造力評分， 然後當然了,

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: who rush in and do everything early are rated as less creative than people who procrastinate moderately. So I want to know what happens to the chronic procrastinators.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 評分往往低於 那些適當拖延的人。 所以我想知道那些慢性拖延症患者 的身上到底發生了什麼。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: She was like, "I don't know. They didn't fill out my survey." (Laughter) No, here are our results. You actually do see that the people who wait until the last minute
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 她說「我不知道，他們沒有填寫調查。」 （笑聲） 開個玩笑，這是我們的結果。 實際上，你確實看到這些 總是等到最後一分鐘的人。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: are so busy goofing off that they don't have any new ideas. And on the flip side, the people who race in are in such a frenzy of anxiety that they don't have original thoughts either.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 總在忙於消磨時間， 以至於他們沒有仍何新的點子。 而另一面，一直在趕工的人， 總是沉浸在緊張與恐慌中， 也沒有任何原創的想法。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: There's a sweet spot where originals seem to live. Why is this? Maybe original people just have bad work habits. Maybe procrastinating does not cause creativity.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 看起來似乎有一個 原創力的最佳甜蜜點。  也許原創性格的人， 只是有不好的工作習慣。 也許拖延並不會引發創造力。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: To find out, we designed some experiments. We asked people to generate new business ideas, and then we get independent readers to evaluate how creative and useful they are.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 為了找到答案，我們設計了一些實驗。 我要求他們創造出新的商業想法， 接著我們找到了一些獨立的讀者， 去評價這個想法的創新性和實用性。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: And some of them are asked to do the task right away. Others we randomly assign to procrastinate by dangling Minesweeper in front of them for either five or 10 minutes.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 然後，有些人會立即 被要求開始做那項任務。 另一群人則被指定拖延。 讓他們先玩玩掃雷， 可能十分鐘，可能十五分鐘。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: And sure enough, the moderate procrastinators are 16 percent more creative than the other two groups. Now, Minesweeper is awesome, but it's not the driver of the effect,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 肯定的，那些適當拖延的人， 創造力比其他兩組高出了百分之十六。 掃雷十分有趣，但卻不是 這種現象的主因，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: because if you play the game first before you learn about the task, there's no creativity boost. It's only when you're told that you're going to be working on this problem,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 因為，如果你在知道這個任務之前 就先玩遊戲， 創造力並不會提高。 只有當你在被告知， 你要去解決這個問題的時候，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and then you start procrastinating, but the task is still active in the back of your mind, that you start to incubate. Procrastination gives you time to consider divergent ideas,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 然後你開始拖延， 但這個任務會在你的腦中運轉著。 然後你開始醞釀， 拖延給了你時間，去發散你的思維，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: to think in nonlinear ways, to make unexpected leaps. So just as we were finishing these experiments, I was starting to write a book about originals,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 用一種非線性的方式去思考， 來達到意想不到的成效。 所以，在我們結束這些實驗的時候， 我開始著手寫一本 有關原創者的書，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and I thought, "This is the perfect time to teach myself to procrastinate, while writing a chapter on procrastination." So I metaprocrastinated, and like any self-respecting precrastinator,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 然後我在想：「當我在寫『拖延』這個章節的時候，  所以我要跟拖延見面， 就像每一個自尊心很強的 提前症患者一樣，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I woke up early the next morning and I made a to-do list with steps on how to procrastinate. (Laughter) And then I worked diligently toward my goal of not making progress toward my goal.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 第二天我就起了個大早， 做了一個如何拖延 的執行清單。  然後我開始認真的去執行 「不做任何進展」的目標。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I started writing the procrastination chapter, and one day -- I was halfway through -- I literally put it away in mid-sentence for months. It was agony.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我開始寫拖延這個章節， 然後有一天，我寫到一半的時候， 我硬是把寫到一半的句子放了下來， 放了一個月。 真是痛苦啊。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: But when I came back to it, I had all sorts of new ideas. As Aaron Sorkin put it, "You call it procrastinating. I call it thinking." And along the way I discovered
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但是當我回到創作中的時候， 腦子裡已經充滿各樣新的想法。 就像Aaron Sorkin所說， 「你說這是拖延，我認為這是思考」。 在創作途中我認識到，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: that a lot of great originals in history were procrastinators. Take Leonardo da Vinci. He toiled on and off for 16 years on the Mona Lisa. He felt like a failure. He wrote as much in his journal.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 歷史上有許多原創的人， 他們都是拖延的人， 例如萊昂納多．達．芬奇。 他在蒙娜麗莎畫像上，  他在自己的日記上常常提到，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: But some of the diversions he took in optics transformed the way that he modeled light and made him into a much better painter. What about Martin Luther King, Jr.?
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但是他分散在光學上的精力， 改變了他形塑光的方式，  馬丁．路德．金呢？

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: The night before the biggest speech of his life, the March on Washington, he was up past 3am, rewriting it. He's sitting in the audience waiting for his turn to go onstage,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 在他生命中規模最大演講的前一夜， 三月的華盛頓， 他凌晨三點鐘起床， 重寫了他的演講稿。 他坐在觀眾席中， 等後輪到他上台的演講，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and he is still scribbling notes and crossing out lines. When he gets onstage, 11 minutes in, he leaves his prepared remarks to utter four words that changed the course of history: "I have a dream."
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 他仍在記著筆記，畫著線。 當他上台後十一分鐘， 他放下了他的筆記， 說出了改變歷史的四個字： 「我有一個夢想」（I have a dream）

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: That was not in the script. By delaying the task of finalizing the speech until the very last minute, he left himself open to the widest range of possible ideas.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 這不在稿子中。 藉由拖延總結演講任務 的最後幾分鐘， 他讓自己置身於最大範圍的想法當中。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: And because the text wasn't set in stone, he had freedom to improvise. Procrastinating is a vice when it comes to productivity, but it can be a virtue for creativity.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 正因為他的稿子十分靈活， 他可以自由的發揮。 當談及生產的時候，拖延是一種惡習， 但對於創造力來說，拖延卻是一種優點。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: What you see with a lot of great originals is that they are quick to start but they're slow to finish. And this is what I missed with Warby Parker. When they were dragging their heels for six months,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 你所看到的許多偉大的原創者， 他們都是迅速的開始，卻緩慢的結束。 這就是為什麼我錯失掉 Warby Parker的原因。 在他們拖沓的六個月中，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I looked at them and said, "You know, a lot of other companies are starting to sell glasses online." They missed the first-mover advantage.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我看著他們然後說， 「你們應該知道，許多其他公司已經 開始在網路上銷售眼鏡了」 他們錯過了搶占先機的優勢。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: But what I didn't realize was they were spending all that time trying to figure out how to get people to be comfortable ordering glasses online.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但我沒意識到的是，他們花了很多時間， 試圖找出讓顧客，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: And it turns out the first-mover advantage is mostly a myth. Look at a classic study of over 50 product categories, comparing the first movers who created the market
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 看這個對五十多種產品的經典研究。 將搶先機創造市場的人

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: with the improvers who introduced something different and better. What you see is that the first movers had a failure rate of 47 percent, compared with only 8 percent for the improvers.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 與那些做出更好改變的改進者相比。 你可以看到率先行動者的 失敗率高達百分之四十七。 相比而言，改進者失敗率只有百分之八。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Look at Facebook, waiting to build a social network until after Myspace and Friendster. Look at Google, waiting for years after Altavista and Yahoo. It's much easier to improve on somebody else's idea
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 看一看Facebook，  再看谷歌，他們是在 Altavista 和 Yahoo 發展幾年之後才創立的。 相比創新的難度，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: than it is to create something new from scratch. So the lesson I learned is that to be original you don't have to be first. You just have to be different and better.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 改進他人的觀點會更加的簡單。 因此我學到的是，要成為一個原創的人， 並不意味著你要成為第一個創新的人。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: But that wasn't the only reason I passed on Warby Parker. They were also full of doubts. They had backup plans lined up, and that made me doubt that they had the courage to be original,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但這不是我拒絕 Warby Parker的唯一原因， 他們滿身都是謎團。 他們已經有後備的計劃， 而這又讓我懷疑，他們有勇氣要原創，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: because I expected that originals would look something like this. (Laughter) Now, on the surface, a lot of original people look confident, but behind the scenes,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 因為這才是我所期望的原創者的樣子。 （笑聲） 現在，從表面上來講， 許多原創的人都非常自信， 但是在看不到的地方，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: they feel the same fear and doubt that the rest of us do. They just manage it differently. Let me show you: this is a depiction of how the creative process works for most of us. (Laughter)
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 他們也感到恐懼和困惑， 就像其他的人一樣。 只是他們處理的方式不同， 讓我告訴你， 這是一個對我們大多數人 創作過程的描述。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Now, in my research, I discovered there are two different kinds of doubt. There's self-doubt and idea doubt. Self-doubt is paralyzing. It leads you to freeze. But idea doubt is energizing.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 在我研究裡，我發現了 兩種不同類型的懷疑。 對自我的懷疑和對想法的懷疑。 對自我的懷疑會讓你麻痺， 它會讓你動彈不得。 但對想法的懷疑會讓你充滿動力，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: It motivates you to test, to experiment, to refine, just like MLK did. And so the key to being original is just a simple thing of avoiding the leap from step three to step four.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 它驅使著你去測試，去實驗，去改善。 就像馬丁路德金所做的一樣， 所以要成為原創的關鍵， 只要簡單的避開掉 階段三到階段四。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Instead of saying, "I'm crap," you say, "The first few drafts are always crap, and I'm just not there yet." So how do you get there? Well, there's a clue, it turns out,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 比起說「我是個廢物」 不如說「一開始的草稿都是廢物， 我只是還沒有達到那個程度罷了。」 所以如何變得具有原創性？ 好，這裡有一個提示，就是，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: in the Internet browser that you use. We can predict your job performance and your commitment just by knowing what web browser you use.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 在你使用的網路瀏覽器中， 我們只要知道你用哪一種網路瀏覽器， 我們就可以預測你的工作表現和你的投入。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Now, some of you are not going to like the results of this study -- (Laughter) But there is good evidence that Firefox and Chrome users significantly outperform Internet Explorer and Safari users. Yes.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 你們有些人可能不會喜歡 這個研究的結果。 （笑聲） 但有聽說，火狐和 谷歌瀏覽器的用戶， 明顯勝過了IE瀏覽器和Safiri瀏覽器。 是的！

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: (Applause) They also stay in their jobs 15 percent longer, by the way. Why? It's not a technical advantage. The four browser groups on average have similar typing speed
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: （掌聲） 另外，他們工作的時間 也多出了百分之十五。 為什麼？這不是因為技術上的優勢。 四個瀏覽器用戶的平均 打字速度都近乎相同，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and they also have similar levels of computer knowledge. It's about how you got the browser. Because if you use Internet Explorer or Safari, those came preinstalled on your computer,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 他們的電腦知識水平也是相同。 這和你如何得到你的瀏覽器有關。 因為如果你用IE或Safari瀏覽器， 它們可能提前就安裝在你的電腦裡了，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and you accepted the default option that was handed to you. If you wanted Firefox or Chrome, you had to doubt the default and ask, is there a different option out there,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 然後你接受了初始設定， 如果你想用火狐或谷歌瀏覽器， 你不得不對初始設定產生懷疑。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and then be a little resourceful and download a new browser. So people hear about this study and they're like, "Great, if I want to get better at my job, I just need to upgrade my browser?" (Laughter)
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 然後你會變得有點鬼靈精怪， 接著又下載了一個新的瀏覽器。 所以，當人們聽到這個研究後，他們說： 「太好了，如果我想在工作中做的更好 我只需要升級我的瀏覽器就好了？」 （笑聲）

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: No, it's about being the kind of person who takes the initiative to doubt the default and look for a better option. And if you do that well, you will open yourself up to the opposite of déjà vu.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 不，這跟你是不是一種 一開始就懷疑初始設定， 並尋找更好選擇的人有關。 如果你能做好這件事。 你將會把自己置於 「似曾相似」的對立面。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: There's a name for it. It's called vuja de. (Laughter) Vuja de is when you look at something you've seen many times before and all of a sudden see it with fresh eyes.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 它有一個名字 叫做vuja de（原詞déjà vu） (笑聲) Vuja de是指，當你在看一個 你之前看過很多次東西的時候， 突然對它產生全新體悟。
