adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: It's a screenwriter who looks at a movie script that can't get the green light for more than half a century. In every past version, the main character has been an evil queen.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 在過去的所有版本中， 主角都是一位邪惡的女王。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: But Jennifer Lee starts to question whether that makes sense. She rewrites the first act, reinvents the villain as a tortured hero and Frozen becomes the most successful animated movie ever.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但 Jennifer Lee開始質疑 這是否合理。 她重寫了第一幕， 將一個壞蛋重塑成了 受盡折磨的英雄，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: So there's a simple message from this story. When you feel doubt, don't let it go. (Laughter) What about fear? Originals feel fear, too. They're afraid of failing,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 這個故事中有一個簡單的信息： 當你感到困惑的時候，不能就這樣算了。 （笑聲）（let it go 是冰雪奇緣主題曲的副歌） 恐懼又怎麼處理呢？ 原創者也會感到恐懼， 他們害怕失敗。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: but what sets them apart from the rest of us is that they're even more afraid of failing to try. They know you can fail by starting a business that goes bankrupt
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但是把他們與我們 其他人不同的地方是， 他們更加害怕沒有嘗試過。 他們知道，你做事業 可能會因為破產而失敗，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: or by failing to start a business at all. They know that in the long run, our biggest regrets are not our actions but our inactions. The things we wish we could redo, if you look at the science,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 或是打從一開始就失敗。   那些我們渴望重新去做的事， 如果你看一下科學界，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: are the chances not taken. Elon Musk told me recently, he didn't expect Tesla to succeed. He was sure the first few SpaceX launches would fail to make it to orbit, let alone get back,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 有那些是沒有把握機會的。 伊隆．馬斯克最近告訴我， 他沒想到特斯拉會成功， 他確認一開始的幾個SpaceX發射計劃， 不會進入正確的軌道，更不用說回收。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: but it was too important not to try. And for so many of us, when we have an important idea, we don't bother to try. But I have some good news for you. You are not going to get judged on your bad ideas.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但是這個計劃太重要了，不嘗試不行。 對於我們絕大多數人來說， 當我們有了一個重要的想法， 我們會不厭其煩的嘗試它。  你將不會再去審視你的糟糕想法。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: A lot of people think they will. If you look across industries and ask people about their biggest idea, their most important suggestion, 85 percent of them stayed silent instead of speaking up.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但大多數的人以為他們會這樣做。 如果你跑遍產業界， 去詢問他們最偉大的想法 和他們最重要的建議。 百分之八十五的人都會選擇沉默， 而非滔滔不絕。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: They were afraid of embarrassing themselves, of looking stupid. But guess what? Originals have lots and lots of bad ideas, tons of them, in fact. Take the guy who invented this.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 他們害怕使自己陷入 看上去很蠢的尷尬。 但是你猜怎麼著？ 原創者有著大量的糟糕想法。 實際上一大堆啊！ 就拿發明這個東西的傢伙舉例。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Do you care that he came up with a talking doll so creepy that it scared not only kids but adults, too? No. You celebrate Thomas Edison for pioneering the light bulb. (Laughter)
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 你會去在意，他發明了一個不僅嚇到小孩，  不！你會為愛迪生發明了燈泡而歡呼。 （笑聲）

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: If you look across fields, the greatest originals are the ones who fail the most, because they're the ones who try the most. Take classical composers, the best of the best.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 如果你去看不同的領域， 最偉大的原創者， 往往是那些失敗最多的人。 因為他們是嘗試最多的人。 就拿古典作曲家來說， 他們是精英中的精英。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Why do some of them get more pages in encyclopedias than others and also have their compositions rerecorded more times? One of the best predictors
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 其中一個最好的猜測是，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: is the sheer volume of compositions that they generate. The more output you churn out, the more variety you get and the better your chances of stumbling on something truly original.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 你越積極的去創作， 你得到的變化也就越多，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Even the three icons of classical music -- Bach, Beethoven, Mozart -- had to generate hundreds and hundreds of compositions to come up with a much smaller number of masterpieces.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 為了創作出幾部音樂傑作，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Now, you may be wondering, how did this guy become great without doing a whole lot? I don't know how Wagner pulled that off. But for most of us, if we want to be more original,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 現在，你可能想知道， 為何這些人幾乎不做任何努力， 就變得如此優秀呢? 我不知道 Wanger 是怎麼做到的。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: we have to generate more ideas. The Warby Parker founders, when they were trying to name their company, they needed something sophisticated, unique, with no negative associations
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我們必須想出更多的點子。 Warby Parker的創立者, 在給他們公司命名的時候， 他們需要一個精緻、獨特

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: to build a retail brand, and they tested over 2,000 possibilities before they finally put together Warby and Parker. So if you put all this together, what you see is that originals
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 在他們嘗試了兩千多種可能後，   如果你把這些事放在一起看，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: are not that different from the rest of us. They feel fear and doubt. They procrastinate. They have bad ideas. And sometimes, it's not in spite of those qualities but because of them that they succeed.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 他們有糟糕的主意。 有時候並不是因為 他們沒有這些缺點，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: So when you see those things, don't make the same mistake I did. Don't write them off. And when that's you, don't count yourself out either. Know that being quick to start but slow to finish
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 所以當你看到這樣的人的時候， 不要和我犯同一個錯誤。  如果這個人是你， 你也不要一開始就認輸，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: can boost your creativity, that you can motivate yourself by doubting your ideas and embracing the fear of failing to try, and that you need a lot of bad ideas in order to get a few good ones.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 可以提高你的創造力，你可以透過懷疑自己的想法和擁抱嘗試失敗的恐懼來激勵自己，並且你需要很多糟糕的想法才能得到一些好的想法。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Look, being original is not easy, but I have no doubt about this: it's the best way to improve the world around us. Thank you. (Applause)
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但我對此深信不疑： 這是我們改變世界最好的方法。 謝謝。 (掌聲)

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: I have a confession to make. But first, I want you to make a little confession to me. In the past year, I want you to just raise your hand if you've experienced relatively little stress. Anyone?
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 如果你曾感受到有一點點壓力的 有嗎？

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: How about a moderate amount of stress? Who has experienced a lot of stress? Yeah. Me too. But that is not my confession. My confession is this: I am a health psychologist,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 那如果說是一般的壓力呢？  沒錯，我跟你們一樣 但這不是我要跟大家坦誠的事

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and my mission is to help people be happier and healthier. But I fear that something I've been teaching for the last 10 years is doing more harm than good, and it has to do with stress.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 我的職責就是要幫助人們 過得更開心也更健康   這跟壓力有關

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: For years I've been telling people, stress makes you sick. It increases the risk of everything from the common cold to cardiovascular disease. Basically, I've turned stress into the enemy.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 它會增加生病的風險 小至一般的小感冒 大至心血管疾病

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: But I have changed my mind about stress, and today, I want to change yours. Let me start with the study that made me rethink my whole approach to stress.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 但我現在對壓力有了不同的看法 而今天我要改變你們的看法 讓我們從這個讓我重新思考 我對於壓力看法的研究報告說起吧

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: This study tracked 30,000 adults in the United States for eight years, and they started by asking people, "How much stress have you experienced in the last year?" They also asked,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 「過去一年裡你承受了多少的壓力？」

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: "Do you believe that stress is harmful for your health?" And then they used public death records to find out who died. (Laughter) Okay. Some bad news first.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 「你認為壓力對你的健康有害嗎？」然後他們利用公開的死亡記錄來找出死亡者。 （笑聲） 好的。首先是一些壞消息。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: People who experienced a lot of stress in the previous year had a 43 percent increased risk of dying. But that was only true for the people who also believed that stress is harmful for your health.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 死亡的風險增加了43％

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: (Laughter) People who experienced a lot of stress but did not view stress as harmful were no more likely to die. In fact, they had the lowest risk of dying of anyone in the study,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 當他們不認為壓力是有害的  事實上，這群人的死亡風險反而是最低的

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: including people who had relatively little stress. Now the researchers estimated that over the eight years they were tracking deaths, 182,000 Americans died prematurely, not from stress,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 研究人員根據過去八年間   死因並非壓力所導致

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: but from the belief that stress is bad for you. (Laughter) That is over 20,000 deaths a year. Now, if that estimate is correct, that would make believing stress is bad for you
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 這個數字顯示出 每年有超過2萬人因此死亡  那麼相信壓力有害身體健康

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: the 15th largest cause of death in the United States last year, killing more people than skin cancer, HIV/AIDS and homicide. (Laughter) You can see why this study freaked me out.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 由此可知為什麼這個研究 會讓我這麼擔憂了

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Here I've been spending so much energy telling people stress is bad for your health. So this study got me wondering: Can changing how you think about stress make you healthier?
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 因為我多年來費盡心力告訴人們   改變你對壓力的想法 就能讓你變得更健康嗎？

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And here the science says yes. When you change your mind about stress, you can change your body's response to stress. Now to explain how this works, I want you all to pretend that you are participants
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 當你改變對壓力的看法 你同時也會改變身體 對於壓力的反應機制  我希望大家假裝自己是這個

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: in a study designed to stress you out. It's called the social stress test. You come into the laboratory, and you're told you have to give a five-minute impromptu speech on your personal weaknesses
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 我希望大家假裝自己是這個 這叫做社會壓力測試 你進到這個實驗室裡 被告知你要針對自己的弱點 進行一場五分鐘的即席演講

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: to a panel of expert evaluators sitting right in front of you, and to make sure you feel the pressure, there are bright lights and a camera in your face, kind of like this. (Laughter)
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 面對一群專業評審 並且為了確保你一定會倍感壓力  大概像現在這樣

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And the evaluators have been trained to give you discouraging, non-verbal feedback, like this. (Exhales) (Laughter) Now that you're sufficiently demoralized, time for part two: a math test.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 就是要給你像這樣洩氣的回應     事情還沒完，下一步是數學測驗

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And unbeknownst to you, the experimenter has been trained to harass you during it. Now we're going to all do this together. It's going to be fun. For me. Okay. (Laughter)
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 當然是在你不知情的情況下 測試者會在過程中間騷擾你

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: I want you all to count backwards from 996 in increments of seven. You're going to do this out loud, as fast as you can, starting with 996. Go! (Audience counting) Go faster. Faster please.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 必須要數出聲來

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: You're going too slow. (Audience counting) Stop. Stop, stop, stop. That guy made a mistake. We are going to have to start all over again. (Laughter) You're not very good at this, are you?
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 那位老兄剛算錯了

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Okay, so you get the idea. If you were actually in this study, you'd probably be a little stressed out. Your heart might be pounding, you might be breathing faster, maybe breaking out into a sweat.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 你可能呼吸也會變得急促 甚至開始冒汗

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And normally, we interpret these physical changes as anxiety or signs that we aren't coping very well with the pressure. But what if you viewed them instead as signs that your body was energized,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 一般來說我們會將這些生理變化 或是我們沒辦法應付壓力的徵兆

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: was preparing you to meet this challenge? Now that is exactly what participants were told in a study conducted at Harvard University. Before they went through the social stress test,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 並且在預備好讓你面對這個挑戰的徵兆呢？  這正是哈佛大學研究調查中 受試者所被告知的

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: they were taught to rethink their stress response as helpful. That pounding heart is preparing you for action. If you're breathing faster, it's no problem. It's getting more oxygen to your brain.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 他們學習到這些壓力反應都是有益的   會讓你的大腦吸到更多氧氣

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And participants who learned to view the stress response as helpful for their performance, well, they were less stressed out, less anxious, more confident, but the most fascinating finding to me
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 當受試者學習將這些抗壓反應機制 視為有助於他們的表現時 他們反而感受到比較少的壓力 但我認為最有趣的發現是

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: was how their physical stress response changed. Now, in a typical stress response, your heart rate goes up, and your blood vessels constrict like this.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 就連他們在生理上的 抗壓反應都隨之改變了  人的心搏率會升高

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And this is one of the reasons that chronic stress is sometimes associated with cardiovascular disease. It's not really healthy to be in this state all the time. But in the study,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 被列為導致心血管疾病原因之一 若血管一直長時間這樣收縮 的確是不太健康的

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: when participants viewed their stress response as helpful, their blood vessels stayed relaxed like this. Their heart was still pounding, but this is a much healthier cardiovascular profile.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 當參與者認為他們的壓力反應有幫助時，他們的血管就會像這樣保持放鬆。他們的心臟仍在跳動，但這是一個更健康的心血管狀況。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: It actually looks a lot like what happens in moments of joy and courage. Over a lifetime of stressful experiences, this one biological change could be the difference
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 看起來他們比較像是 渡過了一段充滿喜樂和勇氣的時光  這一個生理上的改變 可能帶來的影響

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: between a stress-induced heart attack at age 50 and living well into your 90s. And this is really what the new science of stress reveals, that how you think about stress matters.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 將決定你是否會在50歲 就因壓力所導致的心臟病而英年早逝   你看待壓力的方式很重要

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: So my goal as a health psychologist has changed. I no longer want to get rid of your stress. I want to make you better at stress. And we just did a little intervention. If you raised your hand and said
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 我不再希望你去選擇擺脫壓力 我希望你能更善於駕馭壓力 在這裡要打岔一下 如果剛剛你舉手說

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: you'd had a lot of stress in the last year, we could have saved your life, because hopefully the next time your heart is pounding from stress, you're going to remember this talk
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 今天你的人生可能會就此改變 因為希望下次 你會記得今天所聽到的

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and you're going to think to yourself, this is my body helping me rise to this challenge. And when you view stress in that way, your body believes you, and your stress response becomes healthier.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 並會告訴自己 這是我的身體在幫助我面對這個挑戰 當你可以面對壓力可以這樣處之泰然  這樣你面對壓力的反應機制 就也會變得比較健康

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Now I said I have over a decade of demonizing stress to redeem myself from, so we are going to do one more intervention. I want to tell you
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 我剛說我過去十幾年的時間 都把壓力妖魔化 所以我今天要好好彌補一下 現在我們要針對這點再重新思考 我要告訴大家關於抗壓反應 最不為人知的一件事

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: about one of the most under-appreciated aspects of the stress response, and the idea is this: Stress makes you social. To understand this side of stress, we need to talk about a hormone, oxytocin,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 那就是  要想理解關於壓力的這一面

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and I know oxytocin has already gotten as much hype as a hormone can get. It even has its own cute nickname, the cuddle hormone, because it's released when you hug someone.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 我知道催產素作為賀爾蒙的一種 它甚至有個很可愛的小名：抱抱賀爾蒙 會取這名字是因為當你跟人擁抱時 它就會被釋放出來

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: But this is a very small part of what oxytocin is involved in. Oxytocin is a neuro-hormone. It fine-tunes your brain's social instincts. It primes you to do things that strengthen close relationships.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 但這只是催產素功能的一小部分  它可以在腦袋裡微調你的社交本能  好增進你與他人的親密關係

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Oxytocin makes you crave physical contact with your friends and family. It enhances your empathy. It even makes you more willing to help and support the people you care about.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 催產素也會讓你渴望與人有肢體接觸 它也有助於增強同理心 它甚至會讓你更願意去幫助那些 你所關心的人

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Some people have even suggested we should snort oxytocin... to become more compassionate and caring. But here's what most people don't understand about oxytocin. It's a stress hormone.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 但大家不了解關於催產素的是

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Your pituitary gland pumps this stuff out as part of the stress response. It's as much a part of your stress response as the adrenaline that makes your heart pound.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 當身體抗壓機制啓動時 你的腦下垂體就會激生這種激素   就好像腎上腺素會讓你的心臟加快速度一樣

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And when oxytocin is released in the stress response, it is motivating you to seek support. Your biological stress response is nudging you to tell someone how you feel, instead of bottling it up.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 它會促使你去尋求幫助 生理上的抗壓反應 會讓你想要向人傾訴你的感受

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Your stress response wants to make sure you notice when someone else in your life is struggling so that you can support each other. When life is difficult,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 你的抗壓反應機制要確保你會注意到 身邊正在面對一些掙扎的人 好讓你們成為彼此的幫助

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: your stress response wants you to be surrounded by people who care about you. Okay, so how is knowing this side of stress going to make you healthier? Well, oxytocin doesn't only act on your brain.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 那知道了這些可以怎麼幫助我們 更健康地去面對壓力呢

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: It also acts on your body, and one of its main roles in your body is to protect your cardiovascular system from the effects of stress. It's a natural anti-inflammatory.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 它也會在我們的身體裡有所作為  保護我們的心血管系統 它具有純天然的消炎功用

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: It also helps your blood vessels stay relaxed during stress. But my favorite effect on the body is actually on the heart. Your heart has receptors for this hormone,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 它也可以讓血管在感受到 壓力的狀況下依然保持放鬆 但我個人最欣賞它能幫助我們的心臟 你的心臟有一個針對 這種賀爾蒙而設的接收器

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and oxytocin helps heart cells regenerate and heal from any stress-induced damage. This stress hormone strengthens your heart. And the cool thing is that all of these physical benefits
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 跟治療因壓力而造成的受損心細胞

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: of oxytocin are enhanced by social contact and social support. So when you reach out to others under stress, either to seek support or to help someone else, you release more of this hormone,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 不論是想要尋求幫助或是去幫助他人 你的身體都會更多釋放出這種賀爾蒙

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: your stress response becomes healthier, and you actually recover faster from stress. I find this amazing, that your stress response has a built-in mechanism for stress resilience,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 於是你的抗壓機制會變得更為健康 你就也可以更快地從壓力當中解脫  我們體內的抗壓反應有一個 有助於紓緩壓力的內建機制

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and that mechanism is human connection. I want to finish by telling you about one more study. And listen up, because this study could also save a life.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 這個機制就是人際關係。最後我想向大家介紹另一項研究。聽著，因為這項研究也可以救命。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: This study tracked about 1,000 adults in the United States, and they ranged in age from 34 to 93, and they started the study by asking, "How much stress have you experienced in the last year?"
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 年紀為34到93歲的成年人 這份調查中他們會問 「過去一年裡你承受了多少的壓力？」

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: They also asked, "How much time have you spent helping out friends, neighbors, people in your community?" And then they used public records for the next five years to find out who died.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 然後他們又花了接下來五年的時間 追蹤戶政所的死亡紀錄

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Okay, so the bad news first: For every major stressful life experience, like financial difficulties or family crisis, that increased the risk of dying by 30 percent.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 好，我們先講壞消息 我們生命當中可能成為 主要壓力來源的每一個事件 比方說財務困難或家庭風暴 都會讓死亡率上升30個百分點

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: But -- and I hope you are expecting a "but" by now -- but that wasn't true for everyone. People who spent time caring for others showed absolutely no stress-related increase in dying. Zero.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 但是這並不適用於每個人的狀況 那些花時間去關心別人的人 壓力對他們的死亡率不具任何影響 是完全沒有任何影響

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Caring created resilience. And so we see once again that the harmful effects of stress on your health are not inevitable. How you think and how you act can transform your experience of stress.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 壓力對於健康的損害並非不可避免的   可以改變你經歷壓力的方式

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: When you choose to view your stress response as helpful, you create the biology of courage. And when you choose to connect with others under stress, you can create resilience.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 你就能強化抗壓性

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Now I wouldn't necessarily ask for more stressful experiences in my life, but this science has given me a whole new appreciation for stress. Stress gives us access to our hearts.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 但是這項研究確實讓我 對於壓力有了一個全新的認識 壓力讓我們有機會審視自己的心

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: The compassionate heart that finds joy and meaning in connecting with others, and yes, your pounding physical heart, working so hard to give you strength and energy.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 是的，還有你跳得很快的心臟 正在努力地跳動 好供給你能力跟力量

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And when you choose to view stress in this way, you're not just getting better at stress, you're actually making a pretty profound statement.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 當你這麼去看待壓力的時候 你就不會只是在壓力中成長 而是更進一步傳達一個訊息

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: You're saying that you can trust yourself to handle life's challenges. And you're remembering that you don't have to face them alone. Thank you. (Applause)
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 你在說的是，你相信自己 能夠面對生命裡頭的挑戰   （掌聲）

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Chris Anderson: This is kind of amazing, what you're telling us. It seems amazing to me that a belief about stress can make so much difference to someone's life expectancy.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: Chris Anderson： 你剛剛跟我們分享的真是太神奇了  就能對我們的壽命 造成如此大的差異

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: How would that extend to advice, like, if someone is making a lifestyle choice between, say, a stressful job and a non-stressful job, does it matter which way they go?
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 那我們可以如何運用這個概念 並且去給人建議 比方說，如果有人要做 一個生命中的重大決定  他們怎麼選擇會有差嗎？

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: It's equally wise to go for the stressful job so long as you believe that you can handle it, in some sense? KM: Yeah, and one thing we know for certain is that chasing meaning is better for your health
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 即使選擇了壓力會很大的工作

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: than trying to avoid discomfort. And so I would say that's really the best way to make decisions, is go after what it is that creates meaning in your life
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 那就是尋求做有意義的事 比逃避不舒適還更有益於健康 所以我會說 做決定時最好的思考方向

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and then trust yourself to handle the stress that follows. CA: Thank you so much, Kelly. It's pretty cool. (Applause)
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 然後相信自己能夠應付隨之而來的壓力。 CA：非常感謝你，凱利。非常酷。 （掌聲）

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: A few years ago, I felt like I was stuck in a rut, so I decided to follow in the footsteps of the great American philosopher, Morgan Spurlock, and try something new for 30 days.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 幾年之前 所以我決定追隨 美國傑出的哲學家Morgan Spurlock的腳步

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: The idea is actually pretty simple. Think about something you've always wanted to add to your life and try it for the next 30 days. It turns out 30 days is just about the right amount of time
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 這個想法其實非常簡單。想想你一直想為你的生活添加些什麼，並在接下來的 30 天裡嘗試。事實證明 30 天剛好合適

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: to add a new habit or subtract a habit -- like watching the news -- from your life. There's a few things I learned while doing these 30-day challenges. The first was,
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 而我在這三十天的挑戰裡，學到了一些事

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: instead of the months flying by, forgotten, the time was much more memorable. This was part of a challenge I did to take a picture every day for a month.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 和那些匆匆流逝或被遺忘的月份相比 這段時間顯得更彌足珍貴，令人難忘 在這三十天的挑戰裡，每天我要拍攝一張照片

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: And I remember exactly where I was and what I was doing that day. I also noticed that as I started to do more and harder 30-day challenges, my self-confidence grew.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 我清楚地記得那天我在哪裡以及我在做什麼。我還注意到，當我開始進行更多、更難的 30 天挑戰時，我的自信心增強了。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: I went from desk-dwelling computer nerd to the kind of guy who bikes to work. For fun! (Laughter) Even last year, I ended up hiking up Mt. Kilimanjaro, the highest mountain in Africa.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 我從坐在辦公桌前的電腦迷變成了騎自行車上班的人。為了好玩！ （笑聲）去年，我就徒步登上了非洲最高峰乞力馬扎羅山。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: I would never have been that adventurous before I started my 30-day challenges. I also figured out that if you really want something badly enough, you can do anything for 30 days.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 在我開始這三十天的挑戰之前 我也發現

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: Have you ever wanted to write a novel? Every November, tens of thousands of people try to write their own 50,000-word novel, from scratch, in 30 days.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 你曾經想寫一本小說嗎？每年 11 月，成千上萬的人嘗試在 30 天內從頭開始寫出自己的 5 萬字小說。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: It turns out, all you have to do is write 1,667 words a day for a month. So I did. By the way, the secret is not to go to sleep until you've written your words for the day. You might be sleep-deprived,
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 秘密就是除非你能在一天裡寫完1,667個字

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: but you'll finish your novel. Now is my book the next great American novel? No. I wrote it in a month. It's awful. (Laughter) But for the rest of my life, if I meet John Hodgman at a TED party,
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 但你將會完成你的小說   讀起來極其可怕

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: I don't have to say, "I'm a computer scientist." No, no, if I want to, I can say, "I'm a novelist." (Laughter) So here's one last thing I'd like to mention.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 我不一定得說： 「我是一位電腦專家。」 不，如果我想，我能說「我是一位小說家。」

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: I learned that when I made small, sustainable changes, things I could keep doing, they were more likely to stick. There's nothing wrong with big, crazy challenges. In fact, they're a ton of fun.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 我學到了：當我做些小的持續性的改變  這些事比較能夠有始有終  事實上，那些挑戰樂趣無窮

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: But they're less likely to stick. When I gave up sugar for 30 days, day 31 looked like this. (Laughter) So here's my question to you: What are you waiting for? I guarantee you the next 30 days
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 但他們不太可能堅持下去。當我戒糖 30 天時，第 31 天是這樣的。 （笑聲） 所以我問你：你還在等什麼？我向你保證未來30天

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: are going to pass whether you like it or not, so why not think about something you have always wanted to try and give it a shot! For the next 30 days. Thanks. (Applause)

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: When I was 27 years old, I left a very demanding job in management consulting for a job that was even more demanding: teaching. I went to teach seventh graders math in the New York City public schools.
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 辭去了一份很費心血的管理諮詢工作，   教七年級學生數學。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: And like any teacher, I made quizzes and tests. I gave out homework assignments. When the work came back, I calculated grades. What struck me was that IQ was not the only difference
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 也佈置回家作業。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: between my best and my worst students. Some of my strongest performers did not have stratospheric IQ scores. Some of my smartest kids weren't doing so well. And that got me thinking.
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 並不僅僅是智商。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: The kinds of things you need to learn in seventh grade math, sure, they're hard: ratios, decimals, the area of a parallelogram. But these concepts are not impossible,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 當然，你在七年級數學中需要學習的東西是很難的：比率、小數、平行四邊形的面積。但這些概念並非不可能，

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: and I was firmly convinced that every one of my students could learn the material if they worked hard and long enough. After several more years of teaching,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 教了幾年以後，

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: I came to the conclusion that what we need in education is a much better understanding of students and learning from a motivational perspective, from a psychological perspective.
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 我得出的結論是，我們在教育中需要的是更好地理解學生，並從動機和心理學的角度學習。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: In education, the one thing we know how to measure best is IQ. But what if doing well in school and in life depends on much more than your ability to learn quickly and easily? So I left the classroom,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 不僅僅取決於 你是否能又好又快地學習呢？

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: and I went to graduate school to become a psychologist. I started studying kids and adults in all kinds of super challenging settings, and in every study my question was,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 在每次研究中，我關注的是：

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: who is successful here and why? My research team and I went to West Point Military Academy. We tried to predict which cadets would stay in military training and which would drop out.
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 誰在這裡成功了？為什麼？我和我的研究團隊去了西點軍校。我們試著預測哪些學員會繼續接受軍事訓練，哪些會退出。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: We went to the National Spelling Bee and tried to predict which children would advance farthest in competition. We studied rookie teachers working in really tough neighborhoods,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 我們參加了全國拼字比賽，試圖預測哪些孩子會在比賽中取得最大進步。我們研究了在非常艱苦的社區工作的新教師，

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: asking which teachers are still going to be here in teaching by the end of the school year, and of those, who will be the most effective at improving learning outcomes for their students?
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 還能堅持在崗位上。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: We partnered with private companies, asking, which of these salespeople is going to keep their jobs? And who's going to earn the most money? In all those very different contexts,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 我們與私人公司合作，詢問這些銷售人員中哪些人會保住工作？誰會賺最多的錢？在所有這些截然不同的背景下，

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: one characteristic emerged as a significant predictor of success. And it wasn't social intelligence. It wasn't good looks, physical health, and it wasn't IQ. It was grit.
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 有一個特徵成為成功的重要預測因子。這不是社交智力。不是漂亮的外表，不是身體健康，也不是智商。這是砂礫。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: Grit is passion and perseverance for very long-term goals. Grit is having stamina. Grit is sticking with your future, day in, day out, not just for the week, not just for the month, but for years,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 意志力是面對長遠目標時的熱情和毅力。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: and working really hard to make that future a reality. Grit is living life like it's a marathon, not a sprint. A few years ago, I started studying grit in the Chicago public schools.
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 並努力工作，讓這個未來成為現實。堅毅就是讓活著像一場馬拉松，而不是短跑。幾年前，我開始在芝加哥公立學校學習毅力。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: I asked thousands of high school juniors to take grit questionnaires, and then waited around more than a year to see who would graduate. Turns out that grittier kids
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 填寫關於意志力的問卷。 然後等了大約一年多

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: were significantly more likely to graduate, even when I matched them on every characteristic I could measure, things like family income, standardized achievement test scores,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 畢業的可能性明顯越高，

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: even how safe kids felt when they were at school. So it's not just at West Point or the National Spelling Bee that grit matters. It's also in school, especially for kids at risk for dropping out.
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 尤其是對有輟學危險的孩子來說， 意志力同樣重要。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: To me, the most shocking thing about grit is how little we know, how little science knows, about building it. Every day, parents and teachers ask me, "How do I build grit in kids?
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 對於如何鍛煉意志力知之甚少。  "如何鍛煉孩子們的意志力？

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: What do I do to teach kids a solid work ethic? How do I keep them motivated for the long run?" The honest answer is, I don't know. (Laughter) What I do know is that talent doesn't make you gritty.
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 怎樣才能讓他們有長遠的動力？”

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: Our data show very clearly that there are many talented individuals who simply do not follow through on their commitments. In fact, in our data, grit is usually unrelated
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 我們的數據非常清楚地表明，有許多有才華的人根本沒有兌現自己的承諾。事實上，在我們的數據中，毅力通常是無關的

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: or even inversely related to measures of talent. So far, the best idea I've heard about building grit in kids is something called "growth mindset."
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 甚至與人才衡量標準成反比。到目前为止，我听过的关于培养孩子毅力的最好想法就是所谓的“成长心态”。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: This is an idea developed at Stanford University by Carol Dweck, and it is the belief that the ability to learn is not fixed, that it can change with your effort. Dr. Dweck has shown
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 它會由於你的努力發生變化。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: that when kids read and learn about the brain and how it changes and grows in response to challenge, they're much more likely to persevere when they fail,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 他們更有可能在失敗時繼續堅持，

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: because they don't believe that failure is a permanent condition. So growth mindset is a great idea for building grit. But we need more. And that's where I'm going to end my remarks,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 但我們還需要更多這樣的理念。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: because that's where we are. That's the work that stands before us. We need to take our best ideas, our strongest intuitions, and we need to test them. We need to measure whether we've been successful,
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 對他們進行檢驗。

angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.en: and we have to be willing to fail, to be wrong, to start over again with lessons learned. In other words, we need to be gritty about getting our kids grittier. Thank you. (Applause)
angela_lee_duckworth_grit_the_power_of_p.zh: 我們必須願意失敗，願意犯錯，並且學到教訓重新開始。換句話說，我們需要堅韌不拔，讓我們的孩子變得更堅韌。謝謝。 （掌聲）
